difficulty_factors: Chocolate is a triple-threat: cocoa butter (oil), milk protein (if milk chocolate), cocoa tannin
Silk IS protein fiber — protease enzyme will digest the fabric
Must scrape solid first without damaging delicate silk weave
Silk's narrow pH range (4.0–7.0) limits tannin and protein chemistry
Chlorine bleach, ammonia, sodium percarbonate all destroy silk

diy.title: Chocolate on Silk
ingredients: Mild dish soap (1 drop in 1 cup cool water)
mixSteps: Let any melted chocolate solidify first — easier to scrape when solid
Do NOT use enzyme cleaners on silk — silk IS protein
Mix 1 drop mild dish soap into 1 cup cool water
Dilute white vinegar 1:1 with cool water for the tannin step

steps.instruction: Let melted chocolate cool and solidify. Use a dull knife or spoon edge to gently lift solid chocolate off the silk surface. Scrape parallel to the fabric — never dig into it. Remove as much solid material as possible before applying any liquid.
steps.why: Dan Eisen's chocolate procedure and the sequence rules both start the same way: remove solids FIRST. Applying liquid to solid chocolate spreads cocoa butter (fat) deeper into the fiber and expands the stain footprint. Silk is delicate — scrape gently, parallel to the weave.

steps.instruction: Dab the dilute dish soap solution onto the stain with a clean white cloth. Very gently work from outside inward. Let sit 3–5 minutes. Blot with a cloth dampened with cool water.
steps.why: Dish soap's surfactant emulsifies cocoa butter (the fat/oil phase) and loosens any milk protein. On silk, this neutral-pH surfactant replaces the POG solvent + enzyme steps used on cotton — POG can leave rings on silk, and protease enzyme would digest the silk fiber itself. This step addresses BOTH the oil and protein phases simultaneously.

steps.instruction: Dab dilute white vinegar (1:1 with cool water) onto any remaining brown discoloration. Let sit 2–3 minutes. Blot with cool water.
steps.why: The brown color remaining after fat/protein removal is cocoa tannin — polyphenolic pigments from cocoa solids. Dilute acetic acid breaks the hydrogen bonds between these tannins and silk fiber. Dilution is important — undiluted vinegar (pH ~2.4) is below silk's safe range of 4.0–7.0.

steps.instruction: If brown shadow persists, test hydrogen peroxide 3% on a hidden seam. If safe, dab sparingly for 2 minutes maximum. Blot immediately with cool water.
steps.why: Brief peroxide exposure oxidizes residual cocoa tannin chromophore. On silk: 3% only, 2 minutes max, no ammonia catalyst. Extended peroxide contact weakens silk protein structure.

steps.instruction: Lay flat on a clean white towel away from sunlight. Air dry completely. Do NOT apply heat until stain is confirmed gone.
steps.why: Heat sets residual tannin permanently. Sunlight can yellow wet silk. Flat drying prevents water marks.

diy.ifStillThere: Take to a professional dry cleaner who uses GreenEarth solvent (safest for silk). Explain it's chocolate on silk and what you've tried. Professional spotters have controlled-dilution tannin formulas and POG agents that are safe for delicate fabrics when applied correctly.
proTips: NEVER use enzyme cleaners on silk — protease digests silk fibroin.
NEVER use hot water on chocolate stains — heat melts cocoa butter deeper into fiber AND sets milk protein permanently.
Scrape first, chemistry second — always. Liquid on solid chocolate = bigger stain.
If the chocolate is milk chocolate, treat as combination with protein. Dark chocolate has less protein but more tannin.
diy.safety: Test ALL solutions on a hidden seam. Silk dyes bleed easily — if you see color transfer, stop immediately. No chlorine bleach, no ammonia, no enzyme cleaners on silk.
diy.whyThisWorks: Chocolate is a triple-component stain: cocoa butter (oil/fat), milk solids (protein — in milk chocolate), and cocoa polyphenols (tannin). On silk, we use the gentlest possible agents for each phase — surfactant for oil and protein (replacing the POG and enzyme used on cotton), dilute acid for tannin — all within silk's narrow pH window.

home.title: Chocolate on Silk — Careful Removal
home.confidenceNote: Silk severely limits available chemistry. Fresh chocolate with solid scraped off has decent odds. Melted-in or aged chocolate on silk often requires professional treatment.

primaryProduct.name: The Laundress Stain Solution

home.prepStep: Let chocolate solidify, then scrape off solids with a dull knife. NEVER apply liquid to solid chocolate.

method.instruction: Let chocolate solidify. Gently scrape off all solid material with a dull knife or spoon edge. Work parallel to the fabric.
method.why: Removing solids first prevents cocoa butter from spreading deeper into the silk when liquid is applied.

method.instruction: Dab dilute dish soap (1 drop per cup cold water) onto residual stain. Let sit 3–5 minutes. Blot gently with cold water.
method.why: Surfactant emulsifies cocoa butter and loosens milk protein without enzymes or alkali that would damage silk.

method.instruction: Dab dilute white vinegar (1:1 with cold water) onto remaining brown color. Let sit 2–3 minutes. Blot with cold water.
method.why: Acid breaks tannin bonds. Must come after oil/protein step.

method.instruction: Lay flat to air dry. Inspect carefully before any heat or pressing.
method.why: Heat permanently sets residual tannin and can re-melt any remaining cocoa butter.

materialWarnings.warning: NEVER use enzyme cleaners — silk IS protein and protease will dissolve the fiber.
NEVER use ammonia — damages silk protein structure.
NEVER use chlorine bleach — destroys silk instantly.
NEVER use OxiClean (sodium percarbonate) — alkaline pH damages silk.
NEVER use hot water on chocolate — melts cocoa butter deeper into fiber and sets protein.

ifItDoesntWork.nextProduct: Take to a professional dry cleaner
ifItDoesntWork.escalation: Chocolate on silk is a high-difficulty combination. If home treatment doesn't remove it, a professional using GreenEarth solvent and controlled tannin spotting is the safest path.

proTips: Dark chocolate = more tannin, less protein. Milk chocolate = more protein + fat. White chocolate = mostly fat (no tannin).
Cold is your friend — cold water, cold air. No heat until completely clean.
home.whyThisWorks: Chocolate's three components each need different chemistry, but silk's delicacy limits the tools available. Gentle surfactant handles oil and protein, dilute acid handles tannin, all within silk's narrow safety window.

pro.title: Chocolate on Silk — Professional Combination Protocol
pro.stainChemistry: Classification: Combination (oil + protein + tannin). Chocolate contains: (1) Cocoa butter — vegetable fat (triglycerides) that solidifies at room temp. (2) Milk solids (if milk chocolate) — casein protein that coagulates with heat. (3) Cocoa solids — tannin polyphenols (theobroma cacao polyphenols) that oxidize to brown. (4) Starch component — minor, amylase-safe on silk. On silk: protease FORBIDDEN (silk IS fibroin protein). Ammonia FORBIDDEN. Chlorine bleach FORBIDDEN. Sodium percarbonate FORBIDDEN. POG solvents: use with caution — some leave rings on silk. Preferred approach: neutral surfactant for oil+protein, dilute tannin formula for cocoa tannin.

spottingProtocol.technique: Dull spatula parallel to fabric weave
spottingProtocol.instruction: If chocolate is melted, let it cool and solidify first. Scrape solid chocolate from silk surface with a smooth spatula — work parallel to the weave direction, never perpendicular. On silk, the weave is delicate and aggressive scraping causes fiber pulls and snags. Remove maximum solid before any liquid treatment.

spottingProtocol.technique: Gentle water gun at safe distance — no steam
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold water gun from back of fabric, 6+ inches distance. NEVER steam — 280°F sets milk protein permanently and can damage silk. Cold flush removes dissolved cocoa solids and loosens cocoa butter.

spottingProtocol.technique: Emulsify cocoa butter and loosen protein without pH extremes
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply neutral lubricant or nonionic surfactant. This replaces the standard POG + enzyme sequence used on cotton. On silk, nonionic surfactant (A-rated safety) gently emulsifies cocoa butter and loosens casein protein without the alkaline pH of protein formula or the fiber-digesting risk of protease enzyme. Tamp very gently — silk is weak when wet.

spottingProtocol.technique: Clear oil and protein before tannin step
spottingProtocol.instruction: Thorough cold flush. Must clear cocoa butter and protein residue so tannin formula contacts the cocoa pigment directly.

spottingProtocol.technique: Acidic tannin spotter for cocoa polyphenol pigment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply tannin formula diluted 1:1 with water. Silk pH tolerance is 4.0–7.0 — undiluted tannin formula (pH 3–4) risks acid damage. Tamp very gently. The acid breaks hydrogen bonds between cocoa tannin and silk fibroin. Watch for brown color lifting — that's your confirmation. Flush with cold water after 2–3 minutes. Neutralize with neutral lubricant.

spottingProtocol.agent: H₂O₂ 3% — brief contact only, no ammonia
spottingProtocol.technique: Limited oxidation for residual cocoa pigment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only if brown remains after tannin formula. Test 3% H₂O₂ on hidden area. Apply sparingly — no ammonia catalyst on silk. 2 minutes max contact. Flush immediately. If silk shows any yellowing or texture change, stop and flush. Silk's tolerance for oxidation is very limited.

pro.escalation: If stain persists: dry clean in GreenEarth (A-rated for silk) or hydrocarbon solvent. POG pre-spot before solvent cleaning may help with residual cocoa butter. For aged/oxidized cocoa tannin on silk, the stain may be permanent — communicate honestly with the customer.
pro.customerExplanation: Chocolate is three stains in one: cocoa butter (a vegetable fat), milk protein (in milk chocolate), and cocoa pigment (a tannin compound). On silk, we use extra-gentle chemistry because silk itself is a protein fiber — the standard stain-removal agents used on cotton would damage your garment. We treated each component in order with silk-safe agents.
commonMistakes: Using enzyme cleaner on silk — protease digests silk fibroin, creating irreversible damage
Applying liquid before scraping solids — spreads cocoa butter deeper into silk
Using hot water — melts cocoa butter and sets milk protein simultaneously
Using undiluted tannin formula — pH too low for silk, causes acid damage
Using ammonia or alkaline protein formula — damages silk protein and sets tannin
pro.neutralization: After treatment: neutral lubricant to neutralize any acid residue, then cold water flush. Check pH of treated area — should be 5.0–6.5 before drying.
pro.fabricRisks: Silk: protein fiber (fibroin). pH 4.0–7.0 safe. Weak when wet. NEVER: protease, chlorine bleach, ammonia, sodium percarbonate, strong acid. Safest solvents: GreenEarth (A), hydrocarbon (A), Solvon K4 (A), CO₂ (A).
